passages.title: Cloze Test 17-21
passages.text: Cloze Test 1: Renewable energy is becoming increasingly vital for the global economy. (___) the depletion of fossil fuel reserves, nations are investing more in wind and solar power. This transition, (___) , is not without its challenges. The initial costs of infrastructure development can be (___) Moreover, storing energy (___) efficiently remains a technical hurdle. Despite these issues, the shift (___) sustainable sources is inevitable. (___) :

passages.title: Cloze Test 22-26
passages.text: Cloze Test 2: The Renaissance was a period of intense artistic and intellectual activity. (___) in Italy during the 14th century, it soon spread across Europe. This era (___) a move away from medieval scholasticism toward humanism. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci (___) used perspective to create more realistic depictions. Science also flourished, as scholars began to (___) traditional dogmas. (___) its influence, the modern world would look very different today. (___) :

passages.title: Reading 1
passages.text: The Anthropocene epoch is a proposed geological period dating from the commencement of significant human impact on Earth's geology and ecosystems. While the term was coined in the 1960s, it gained widespread prominence in 2000 through the work of Paul Crutzen. The primary argument for this new designation is that human activities, particularly since the Industrial Revolution, have altered the carbon cycle, nitrogen cycle, and global climate to such an extent that they leave a permanent mark in the lithosphere. However, critics argue that the term is more political than scientific, suggesting that the Holocene—the current epoch—already encompasses human civilization.

passages.title: Reading 2
passages.text: Behavioral economics challenges the traditional economic assumption that humans are rational actors who always seek to maximize their utility. Instead, it incorporates insights from psychology to explain why people often make systematic errors in judgment. Concepts such as loss aversion—the idea that the pain of losing is twice as powerful as the joy of gaining—and the endowment effect demonstrate that our decisions are frequently driven by cognitive biases rather than logic. By understanding these irrationalities, policymakers can design 'nudges' that steer people toward better choices without restricting their freedom. The passage implies that traditional economics...

passages.title: Reading 3
passages.text: The Library of Alexandria was one of the largest and most significant libraries of the ancient world. Dedicated to the Muses, it functioned as a major center for scholarship from its construction in the 3rd century BC until the Roman conquest of Egypt. The library's goal was to collect all the world's knowledge, and at its height, it may have held hundreds of thousands of papyrus scrolls. However, its destruction is a subject of historical debate, with theories blaming Julius Caesar, the Caliph Omar, or internal decay and budget cuts. Regardless of the cause, its loss represents a profound gap in our understanding of ancient history. The main focus of the passage is...

passages.title: Reading 4
passages.text: Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter that is thought to account for approximately 85% of the matter in the universe. Its presence is implied in a variety of astrophysical observations, including gravitational effects that cannot be explained by accepted theories of gravity unless more matter is present than can be seen. Because dark matter does not appear to interact with the electromagnetic field, it does not absorb, reflect, or emit light, making it extremely difficult to detect. Astronomers infer its existence from the motion of stars and galaxies. Scientists are currently using particle accelerators and deep underground detectors to identify the particles that constitute it.

passages.title: Reading 5
passages.text: The concept of 'biophilia' suggests that humans possess an innate tendency to seek connections with nature and other forms of life. Edward O. Wilson, who popularized the term, argued that this bond is a product of biological evolution. For most of human history, our ancestors lived in close contact with the natural world, and those who could read nature's cues were more likely to survive. Today, research in environmental psychology supports this, showing that access to green spaces can reduce stress and improve cognitive function. Despite urbanization, the psychological need for nature remains a fundamental part of the human condition.

questions.question:
Kevin: I’ve been reading about Universal Basic Income lately. It seems like a radical way to address poverty.
Sarah: It is, but many economists worry about the inflation it might trigger.
Kevin: __________________
Sarah: That’s a fair point, but finding a balance between support and economic stability is the real challenge.

options.A: Inflation is inevitable regardless of what the government does.
options.B: But wouldn't the increase in consumer spending boost the economy in the long run?
options.C: I think we should just lower taxes instead.
options.D: I don't believe poverty can ever be truly eliminated.
options.E: Why are economists always so pessimistic about social welfare?

questions.question:
Professor: The deadline for the dissertation proposal is next Friday. No exceptions.
Student: __________________
Professor: I understand, but the schedule is tight because of the upcoming symposium. Try to focus on the core thesis for now.

options.A: I have already finished my research, so I am ready.
options.B: Will we be graded on the symposium as well?
options.C: Is there any way I could get a short extension due to my illness?
options.D: Can I change my topic after the deadline?
options.E: I am planning to submit it by Wednesday.

questions.question:
Journalist: Minister, how does the government plan to handle the rising energy costs this winter?
Minister: We are implementing a series of subsidies for low-income households.
Journalist: __________________
Minister: We are currently negotiating with international suppliers to diversify our energy sources.

options.A: Will those subsidies be enough to cover the increase?
options.B: That sounds good, but what about the long-term energy security?
options.C: Energy costs are rising all over the world, aren't they?
options.D: Why didn't you invest in solar energy ten years ago?
options.E: When will the subsidies be distributed?

questions.question:
Client: I'm not sure if this software update is worth the cost.
Consultant: It includes advanced security protocols that protect against the latest cyber threats.
Client: __________________
Consultant: Precisely. A single data breach could cost you much more than the update fee.

options.A: I think my current firewall is sufficient.
options.B: Is the interface easier to use than the old version?
options.C: So, you're saying it's more of a preventative investment?
options.D: How long will the installation process take?
options.E: Can I get a discount if I buy multiple licenses?

questions.question:
Amy: Did you see the latest findings on the Mars rover mission?
Mark: Yes, the evidence of ancient organic molecules is groundbreaking.
Amy: __________________
Mark: Exactly. It doesn't prove there was life, but it shows the ingredients were there.

options.A: I wonder if we will ever send humans to Mars.
options.B: It brings us one step closer to understanding if Mars was once habitable.
options.C: The rover mission has been very expensive for taxpayers.
options.D: I think the moon mission was more significant.
options.E: Organic molecules are common in the universe, though.

questions.question: Despite the fierce competition in the smartphone market, the company managed to increase its market share by focusing on niche consumer needs.

options.A: The company failed to grow because the competition in the smartphone market was too intense.
options.B: By targeting specific customer requirements, the company expanded its share of the market even though competition was tough.
options.C: The company decided to ignore the competition and focus only on high-end luxury smartphones.
options.D: Market share increase was inevitable for the company due to its focus on niche products despite no competition.
options.E: The fierce competition in the market led the company to abandon its focus on niche consumers.

questions.question: Hardly had the new law been enacted when the public began to voice their dissatisfaction through various protests.

options.A: The public protested for a long time before the new law was finally passed.
options.B: As soon as the new law came into effect, people started expressing their unhappiness via protests.
options.C: The new law was enacted because the public had been protesting for months.
options.D: The government expected protests, so they delayed the enactment of the law.
options.E: Dissatisfaction with the law was only expressed after it had been in place for several years.

questions.question: It is not so much the lack of talent as the lack of persistence that prevents many artists from achieving success.

options.A: Many artists fail to succeed because they are neither talented nor persistent.
options.B: Talent is far more important than persistence for an artist who wants to be successful.
options.C: Most artists fail because they don't have enough talent, regardless of how persistent they are.
options.D: The failure of many artists to succeed is due more to their lack of perseverance than a lack of ability.
options.E: If an artist is talented enough, they do not need to be persistent to achieve success.

questions.question: Were it not for the timely intervention of the rescue teams, the casualties from the flood would have been much higher.

options.A: The rescue teams arrived too late to prevent a high number of casualties during the flood.
options.B: Even with the rescue teams' help, the number of casualties remained significantly high.
options.C: The flood caused many deaths because the rescue teams were unable to intervene.
options.D: If the rescue teams had not intervened when they did, more people would have died in the flood.
options.E: The rescue teams intervened only after the number of casualties had already peaked.

questions.question: The concept of sustainable development has evolved significantly since the 1980s. Initially, it was seen primarily as an environmental issue, focusing on the conservation of natural resources. __________________. Today, it is understood as a multidimensional approach that balances economic growth, social equity, and environmental protection.

options.A: However, the Industrial Revolution changed the way we perceive nature.
options.B: Furthermore, many countries still refuse to sign environmental treaties.
options.C: However, over time, the definition expanded to include social and economic dimensions.
options.D: This led to the immediate cessation of all deforestation worldwide.
options.E: Environmentalists argue that the economy should be the only priority.

questions.question: In the realm of quantum mechanics, particles can exist in a state of superposition. This means that a particle can be in multiple states at the same time until it is measured. __________________. Once an observation is made, the wave function collapses, and the particle settles into a single, definite state.

options.A: This phenomenon is the basis for traditional computing systems.
options.B: Measurement, therefore, plays a fundamental role in determining reality at the quantum level.
options.C: Albert Einstein was the first to successfully measure a particle in superposition.
options.D: Most particles are too large to be affected by these quantum laws.
options.E: The collapse of the wave function is a myth that has been debunked by modern physics.

questions.question: The rise of remote work has fundamentally altered the corporate landscape. Companies have realized that employees can be just as productive, if not more so, from their homes. __________________. Consequently, many firms are downsizing their physical office spaces to reduce overhead costs.

options.A: Commuting to work is still considered the most efficient way to start the day.
options.B: This shift has led to a decrease in the demand for commercial real estate in city centers.
options.C: Employees often prefer to work in loud, collaborative environments.
options.D: Remote work is only possible for those in the manufacturing sector.
options.E: The internet has little to do with the success of remote working models.

questions.question: Standardized testing has long been a staple of the educational system, intended to provide an objective measure of student achievement. __________________. Critics argue that these tests often measure a student's ability to memorize facts rather than their critical thinking or creativity.

options.A: However, its efficacy and fairness have come under increasing scrutiny in recent years.
options.B: Therefore, all universities should rely solely on these test scores for admissions.
options.C: Students who score well on these tests are guaranteed to succeed in their careers.
options.D: The history of standardized testing dates back to ancient China.
options.E: Teachers generally prefer standardized tests because they are easy to grade.

questions.question: (I) Genetic engineering has the potential to revolutionize medicine by allowing for the correction of defective genes. (II) Researchers are currently exploring ways to use CRISPR technology to treat hereditary diseases. (III) However, there are significant ethical concerns regarding the 'editing' of human embryos. (IV) Most genetically modified crops are engineered to be resistant to pests and herbicides. (V) These moral dilemmas must be addressed before the technology becomes a standard medical practice.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) The Renaissance was a period of intense artistic and intellectual activity that began in Italy. (II) It marked the transition from the Middle Ages to modernity during the 15th and 16th centuries. (III) Michelangelo and Leonardo da Vinci are perhaps the most famous figures of this era. (IV) Modern digital art has changed the way we perceive traditional painting techniques. (V) This cultural movement eventually spread across Europe, influencing literature, science, and politics.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Urban heat islands occur when cities replace natural land cover with dense concentrations of pavement and buildings. (II) These surfaces absorb and retain heat, leading to higher temperatures in urban areas compared to rural surroundings. (III) Planting trees and installing green roofs can help mitigate this effect. (IV) Air conditioning is often expensive for low-income families living in city apartments. (V) Reducing the number of dark-colored surfaces also plays a key role in cooling urban environments.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) The Great Depression was the worst economic downturn in the history of the industrialized world. (II) It began after the stock market crash of October 1929, which sent Wall Street into a panic. (III) During this time, consumer spending dropped and investment declined, causing steep declines in industrial output. (IV) Many people today use credit cards for their daily shopping needs. (V) By 1933, when the Depression reached its lowest point, some 15 million Americans were unemployed.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Volcanic eruptions are among the most powerful and destructive natural events on Earth. (II) They occur when magma from within the Earth's upper mantle works its way to the surface. (III) At the surface, it erupts to form lava flows and ash deposits. (IV) Geologists use seismographs to monitor the movement of tectonic plates. (V) These eruptions can significantly alter the surrounding landscape and even affect the global climate.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V
